content.text: SBOM Generation for Claude Application Dependencies
A
Software Bill of Materials (SBOM)

is a machine-readable inventory of every package, library, and transitive dependency included in a software artifact. For a Claude application, this means the Anthropic SDK, HTTP clients, validation libraries, and their full dependency trees — documented at each build so that when a new CVE is disclosed, you can answer within minutes: 'does our deployed version include this package?'

An SBOM answers the CVE question retrospectively. Without one, a new vulnerability announcement triggers a manual audit of every deployed version. With one, you query the SBOM index and know in seconds.

This sub-subtopic covers how to generate CycloneDX and SPDX SBOMs using Syft, scan them for known vulnerabilities with Grype, attach them to OCI image digests, and continuously monitor for new disclosures against deployed versions.

attrs.alt: SBOM generation pipeline: Syft scan → CycloneDX/SPDX artifact → Grype CVE scan → OCI attachment + Cosign attestation + Dependency Track monitoring

content.text: Generating the SBOM with Syft
Syft is an open-source SBOM generator that reads Python package metadata, container image layers, and lock files. Run it against the installed environment to capture
all transitive dependencies
, not just top-level requirements:

# Install Syft (pinned version for reproducibility)
curl -sSfL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anchore/syft/main/install.sh \
  | sh -s -- -b /usr/local/bin v1.4.1

# Generate CycloneDX JSON SBOM from the local Python environment
syft packages python:. \
  -o cyclonedx-json=sbom.cdx.json \
  -o spdx-json=sbom.spdx.json \
  --file sbom.cdx.json

# Generate from a built Docker image (captures OS packages too)
syft packages ghcr.io/org/claude-api:sha256-abc123... \
  -o cyclonedx-json=sbom.cdx.json
The CycloneDX JSON format is the most widely supported for tooling integration. The output includes component name, version, PURL (Package URL), license, and hash for every package:
Scanning the SBOM for CVEs with Grype
Grype matches the SBOM's component list against the NVD, GitHub Advisory, and OS vendor vulnerability databases. Run it immediately after Syft in the CI pipeline:

# Install Grype (pinned version)
curl -sSfL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anchore/grype/main/install.sh \
  | sh -s -- -b /usr/local/bin v0.78.0

# Scan the SBOM (faster than rescanning the image)
grype sbom:sbom.cdx.json --output json > vuln-report.json

# Summary view
grype sbom:sbom.cdx.json

# Example output:
# NAME       INSTALLED  FIXED-IN  TYPE    VULNERABILITY   SEVERITY
# httpx      0.26.0     0.27.0    python  GHSA-xxx-yyy    High
# certifi    2023.11.0  2024.1.0  python  CVE-2024-xxxx   Medium

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# ci_vuln_gate.sh — block the build on CRITICAL, warn on HIGH
set -euo pipefail

grype sbom:sbom.cdx.json --output json > vuln-report.json

CRITICAL=$(jq '[.matches[] | select(.vulnerability.severity == "Critical")] | length' vuln-report.json)
HIGH=$(jq '[.matches[] | select(.vulnerability.severity == "High")] | length' vuln-report.json)

echo "Vulnerability scan: ${CRITICAL} CRITICAL, ${HIGH} HIGH"

if [ "${CRITICAL}" -gt 0 ]; then
  echo "FAIL: ${CRITICAL} CRITICAL vulnerability(ies) found — build blocked"
  jq -r '.matches[] | select(.vulnerability.severity == "Critical") |
    "  \(.artifact.name)@\(.artifact.version): \(.vulnerability.id) (\(.vulnerability.severity))"' \
    vuln-report.json
  exit 1
fi

if [ "${HIGH}" -gt 0 ]; then
  echo "WARN: ${HIGH} HIGH vulnerability(ies) — review required before deploy"
fi

echo "Vuln gate passed."
Integrating SBOM Generation into GitHub Actions

# .github/workflows/build.yml — SBOM generation and vuln scan
name: Build and SBOM

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:

jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      contents: read
      packages: write
      id-token: write   # for Cosign OIDC signing

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Set up Python
        uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: '3.12'

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: pip install -r requirements.lock

      - name: Build Docker image
        run: |
          docker build -t ghcr.io/${{ github.repository }}:${{ github.sha }} .

      - name: Generate SBOM with Syft
        uses: anchore/sbom-action@v0
        with:
          image: ghcr.io/${{ github.repository }}:${{ github.sha }}
          format: cyclonedx-json
          output-file: sbom.cdx.json

      - name: Scan SBOM with Grype
        uses: anchore/scan-action@v3
        with:
          sbom: sbom.cdx.json
          fail-build: true
          severity-cutoff: critical

      - name: Push image
        run: docker push ghcr.io/${{ github.repository }}:${{ github.sha }}

      - name: Attach SBOM to image with Cosign
        uses: sigstore/cosign-installer@v3
        # Runs the next step after install
      - run: |
          cosign attach sbom \
            --sbom sbom.cdx.json \
            --type cyclonedx \
            ghcr.io/${{ github.repository }}:${{ github.sha }}

          # Also sign with keyless OIDC
          cosign sign --yes \
            ghcr.io/${{ github.repository }}:${{ github.sha }}

      - name: Upload SBOM as artifact
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: sbom-${{ github.sha }}
          path: sbom.cdx.json
          retention-days: 365
Continuous Monitoring with Dependency Track
Generating an SBOM at build time is valuable;
continuously re-scanning

deployed SBOMs against the latest CVE databases is more valuable. Dependency Track is an open-source platform that ingests SBOMs and alerts when new vulnerabilities are disclosed for packages already in production:

#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""upload_sbom_to_deptrack.py — upload SBOM to Dependency Track after deploy."""
import os, sys, json
import requests

DT_URL     = os.environ["DEPENDENCY_TRACK_URL"]    # e.g. https://deptrack.example.com
DT_API_KEY = os.environ["DEPENDENCY_TRACK_API_KEY"]
PROJECT    = os.environ["DT_PROJECT_NAME"]          # e.g. claude-api
VERSION    = os.environ.get("IMAGE_TAG", "unknown")
SBOM_FILE  = sys.argv[1] if len(sys.argv) > 1 else "sbom.cdx.json"

def upload(sbom_path: str, project: str, version: str) -> dict:
    with open(sbom_path, "rb") as f:
        sbom_bytes = f.read()

    resp = requests.put(
        f"{DT_URL}/api/v1/bom",
        headers={"X-Api-Key": DT_API_KEY, "Content-Type": "application/json"},
        json={
            "projectName": project,
            "projectVersion": version,
            "autoCreate": True,
            "bom": sbom_bytes.decode(),
        },
    )
    resp.raise_for_status()
    return resp.json()

if __name__ == "__main__":
    result = upload(SBOM_FILE, PROJECT, VERSION)
    print(f"SBOM uploaded: token={result.get('token')}")
    print(f"View at: {DT_URL}/projects")

Dependency Track sends alerts (email, Slack, webhook) when a new CVE is published for any component in any tracked SBOM version. This means a vulnerability disclosed 6 months after your deploy will still trigger an alert — without regenerating the SBOM.
Querying the SBOM for CVE Impact
When a CVE is announced, the first question is: which deployed versions include the affected package? A simple script queries the stored SBOM:
Summary
Syft
generates CycloneDX/SPDX SBOMs from Python environments or OCI images, capturing all transitive dependencies with PURLs and hashes
Grype
scans the SBOM against NVD and GitHub Advisory databases; CI blocks on CRITICAL, warns on HIGH
GitHub Actions integration
runs Syft → Grype → Cosign attach in every build, producing a signed SBOM attached to the image digest
Dependency Track
continuously re-scans uploaded SBOMs against new CVE disclosures — a vulnerability announced months later still triggers an alert
SBOM query script
answers 'which deployed versions include httpx < 0.27.0?' in seconds rather than requiring a manual audit of every Dockerfile
Cosign attestation
creates a verifiable, tamper-evident link between the SBOM and the image digest via Sigstore's keyless signing infrastructure
